findings.reachability: Reachable on any product search page where the WOOF price filter widget is rendered. Exploitation requires a second plugin or theme hooking 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids' and returning non-integer array elements, or a future regression in the upstream populator. The filter is public and documented.
findings.justification: At line 109, the output of apply_filters('dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids', []) is passed directly to implode(',', $post_ids) and concatenated into a raw SQL string: `$sql .= " AND $wpdb->posts.ID IN(" . implode( ',', $post_ids ) . ')'`. No integer cast (array_map('intval',...) or absint()) is applied, nor is $wpdb->prepare() used. Any WordPress hook registered on this public filter can inject non-integer elements (e.g., '1 UNION SELECT...') that break out of the IN() clause.

detection_pattern.section_name: SQL Injection: apply_filters array imploded into IN() clause without integer cast
detection_pattern.description: Catches PHP code that calls apply_filters() to obtain an array and immediately implodes it into a SQL fragment without integer sanitization. WordPress filters are public extension points; any plugin can alter the returned array. Without array_map('intval',...) or $wpdb->prepare(), SQL injection is possible via injected non-integer elements.
